advantages: Decompose multivariable sensitivity into per-coordinate rates of change, making high-dimensional functions tractable one variable at a time.
Provide the components needed to build the gradient vector for optimization, backpropagation and automatic differentiation.
Enable local linear approximations (tangent planes) that justify first-order optimization steps and Taylor expansions.
Support interpretability: partials with respect to inputs reveal which features drive model outputs at a specific point.
disadvantages: Only capture local, first-order behavior—large parameter or input changes can violate the linear approximation.
The number of first-order partials grows linearly with dimension; higher-order or mixed partials multiply bookkeeping cost.
Holding other variables fixed ignores simultaneous coordinated changes, which can mislead when inputs are strongly correlated.
Numerical finite-difference partials in high dimensions are expensive and noisy compared to analytic or autograd computation.
